results.title: Gemini Spark – Your 24/7 personal AI agent for productivity
results.content:
Try Gemini

# Gemini Spark Your 24/7 personal AI agent.

Gemini Spark helps you navigate your digital life. Give it a task and it works in the background 24/7, even if your phone and laptop are turned off. It operates autonomously, but always under your direction. You choose to turn it on and it's designed to check with you before taking major actions.

Help me find and track interior design internships in New Orleans for this summer.

Help me find and track interior design internships in New Orleans for this summer.

 

Every Monday at 9:00 AM, scan my inbox and review my emails from the past week. Give me a quick recap of the most important updates and provide a suggested, prioritized to-do list for this week. Also schedule some calendar blocks for deep work. [...] Not at all. We designed Gemini Spark to be an accessible AI agent for everyone. You interact with it using regular conversation. Whether you're setting up complex, multi-app schedules or just using it as a personal AI agent for your daily tasks, no technical expertise is required.

Check responses. Supervise closely, interrupt when needed. Subscription required. Compatibility and availability vary. 18+. [...] While standard chatbots wait for a prompt, Gemini Spark can take proactive action on your behalf, under your direction. It doesn't just answer questions, it completes multi-step tasks across your applications making it a true AI agent rather than just a conversational tool.

Absolutely. It is a highly capable AI agent for email management. You can instruct it to draft replies, sort incoming messages based on priority, or extract action items from long threads, making it the perfect AI agent for personal use when you're overwhelmed by unread messages.

results.title: What is Google Gemini Spark?Your 24/7 personal AI agent.
results.content:
The learning part sounds fancy but it’s straightforward.

Spark does not retrain its underlying AI on your data. That would be expensive and slow. Instead it builds a separate layer of context about you. This layer sits on top of the base model and tells it how you specifically do things.

What this means in practice:

   The base model knows how to draft an email
   Your personal layer knows you start emails with “Hi” not “Hello”
   Your personal layer knows you never use exclamation points
   Your personal layer knows you copy your manager on anything about budget

Over time this personal layer gets more accurate. Spark’s actions feel less like a robot and more like you.

### Privacy Questions

A thing that watches everything you do will make you nervous. It should. [...] 💻What is Ollama: Running Large Language Models Locally

Text to speech [...] Gmail (drafting, filtering, summarizing)
   Google Calendar (scheduling, reminders, conflict detection)
   Google Drive and Docs (organization, content generation)
   Google Meet (transcription, summaries, follow-up tasks)
   Google Tasks and Keep (contextual to-dos)

If you live in Google Workspace, Spark lives there too.

### Third Party Connections

External tools come next. Slack, Salesforce, Notion.

The early integrations can read data from these tools. Writing to them or triggering actions inside them is harder. That will come later.

The real test is whether Spark can truly act across the tools you actually use, not just the ones Google owns.

### The Bigger Picture

Spark is not Google’s only agent. They have several.

Image 24: David

The "24/7 even with your phone off" plus "checks with you before major actions" is a real tension, and it's the interesting part. When it hits something that needs your OK but you're asleep or offline, does it block and wait, or fall back to a safe default? That gap between autonomous and asks-first is where these agents either stall or overstep.

results.title: How to build a 24/7 personal AI agent with Claude Code. Step-by-step guide for non-technical users. · GitHub
results.content:
### If Something Breaks

Open Claude Code (or SSH in from your Mac) and describe the problem:

"I can't connect to the remote desktop. Help me diagnose why."

"Obsidian won't launch. Fix it."

"The server feels slow. Check what's using memory and CPU."

Claude Code can read logs, check processes, and fix most issues. That's the whole point of having it on the server.

### Full Setup Health Check

Run this to verify everything is working, or ask Claude Code to run it and explain the results:

Everything should show "active (running)" or return sensible values. If anything shows "inactive", "failed", or is missing, ask Claude Code to diagnose it. With this guide in your workspace, it has all the context it needs to help. [...] The context problem. Every new chat starts from zero. The AI doesn't know my notes, my preferences, my projects. I'd spend the first five minutes of every session re-explaining who I am and what I'm working on.

The autonomy problem. Tools like OpenClaw promise a fully autonomous AI agent. And they're impressive. But autonomy without control is a liability. Tasks loop. Reasoning wanders. Outcomes need manual review. I wanted an agent that could act on my behalf but still asked before doing anything irreversible. [...] You don't need a Mac Mini. The OpenClaw trend has people buying $600+ Mac Minis to run a 24/7 agent. That makes sense if you're running local AI models to avoid API costs. But if you're using Claude, the model runs on Anthropic's servers regardless. A $10/month VPS does the same job. No hardware investment, no home network dependency, no worrying about power cuts.

Controlled autonomy. Claude Code asks before it acts. It shows you what it's about to do and waits for permission. OpenClaw defaults to high autonomy, which is powerful, but its over-autonomy is a documented problem. Tasks get stuck in loops, reasoning wanders, and outcomes can be unpredictable. I'd rather approve an action in 2 seconds than spend 20 minutes undoing one.
